typicalStructure.whatItDoes: Address the recipient and state in 1 sentence why you're writing.
sampleOpeners: Dear Mr Lim, I am writing to request permission to make an announcement...
Hi Aisha, How are you? I hope you are doing well!

typicalStructure.paragraphLabel: 2. The required facts (audience info, what / when / where)
typicalStructure.whatItDoes: Deliver the must-include facts crisply and clearly.
sampleOpeners: The event will be held on 11 October 2023, in the school hall, at 2 p.m.
It is on Saturday, 25 November 2017 at the Singapore Zoo from 8 a.m. to 11 a.m.

typicalStructure.paragraphLabel: 3. The 'reason' paragraph — WHY the audience should agree / care
typicalStructure.whatItDoes: This is where the writing earns its marks — go beyond facts to give compelling reasons, explain the 'why', and persuade the audience.
sampleOpeners: I originally planned to watch the movie with my Uncle Tim but...
As my classmates and I will be posted to our secondary schools in just three months, we would like to...

typicalStructure.whatItDoes: Ask politely for what you want, state deadlines if any, thank them, and sign off appropriately based on the register.
sampleOpeners: I would really appreciate your help in this matter and I look forward to your favourable reply!
Do let us know before then if you would be keen to take part... Yours sincerely,

keyInfoChecklist: Address the audience by name with appropriate formality (Dear Mr/Mrs vs Hi)
State your purpose in the opening line — don't bury it
Cover EVERY required fact from the prompt (typically 3 — what / when / where, or who / why / how)
Match register to audience (formal for principal/teacher, friendly for peer)
Stay within the word count (usually 100-150 words)
End with a polite call to action AND a sign-off
reasonParagraphOverview: The 'reason' paragraph is the heart of situational writing. It transforms a robotic checklist of facts into a genuinely persuasive or informative piece. A 4-mark answer merely lists the required points, while a 6-mark answer seamlessly weaves these facts into a compelling narrative that directly addresses the audience's needs, emotions, or interests.

reasonParagraphMoves.move: Show the benefit to the audience, not just to yourself
reasonParagraphMoves.why: Persuasion lands when the AUDIENCE sees what's in it for them.
examples: From 2022: 'Running is a great way to decompress, and this would be a good chance for us to relax after the PSLE.'
From 2017: '...this competition will be a piece of cake for you since you love painting animals. Also, there are attractive prizes...'

reasonParagraphMoves.move: Connect the action to a meaningful cause or shared history
reasonParagraphMoves.why: Appealing to shared values or past experiences makes the request highly personal and harder to refuse.
examples: From 2021: '...we would like to do something special for all our teachers to show our appreciation for what they have done...'
From 2023: 'Remember when you complained about the school library needing more books? Now is the perfect chance to increase the number...'

reasonParagraphMoves.move: Highlight the audience's specific skills or traits
reasonParagraphMoves.why: Flattery and recognition of competence build confidence and make the audience feel uniquely qualified.
examples: From 2020: 'Your cake at our last baking class was the best. We think you are a fantastic baker and would be a valuable asset...'
From 2024: 'We heard that you helped her to rehearse and I would love to receive your help too, as I believe that it would give me the confidence...'

reasonParagraphMoves.move: Explain the 'why' behind a change of plans or a specific need
reasonParagraphMoves.why: Providing context for a sudden request or change builds trust and clarifies the situation.
examples: From 2019: 'I originally planned to watch the movie with my Uncle Tim but his company needs him to travel overseas next week...'
From 2018: 'Gina asked John to let her purchase her food first as she was really hungry. However, John refused...'

reasonParagraphMoves.move: End the paragraph with the emotional payoff
reasonParagraphMoves.why: Leave the reader feeling something before the call to action, cementing their desire to agree.
examples: From 2023: 'It is truly a meaningful event that I do not want us to miss out on...'
From 2025: 'One big way I have benefitted from basketball is that it has made me more confident.'

reasonParagraphPhrases.name: Open the reason paragraph
phrases: I am writing to ask you because ...
The main reason I'm asking is that ...
What makes this special is that ...

reasonParagraphPhrases.name: Acknowledge / pre-empt objections
phrases: I understand you might be worried that ...
I know this might seem like a big ask, however ...

reasonParagraphPhrases.name: Show benefit / 'what's in it for them'
phrases: This would mean a lot to ...
You would be giving ... a chance to ...

reasonParagraphPhrases.name: Land the emotional payoff
phrases: It would mean the world to ...
I cannot think of anyone better suited than ...
